aboutPage.metaTitle: About the Project | Metro High School Advocacy
aboutPage.metaDescription: A student-led advocacy initiative at Metro Academic & Classical High School addressing the STL recycling crisis.
aboutPage.title: About the Project
aboutPage.tagline: A student-led advocacy initiative at Metro Academic & Classical High School.
aboutPage.missionBody:
This website is the culmination of our Advocacy Project for Mr. Boyle’s class. Our goal is to address the critical breakdown of the recycling infrastructure in St. Louis. By synthesizing academic research, city policy, and direct interviews, we aim to provide a clear roadmap for residents to navigate the current 'Drop-Off' model.

As per the project requirements, we have curated 15 high-quality academic sources and conducted a primary interview with a city authority to ensure our information is both accurate and actionable.
aboutPage.contextQuote: "Explore and research a cause you care deeply about, find an authority figure to interview, and raise awareness for the issue by publishing research in a meaningful way."

metadata.metaTitle: Recycle St. Louis | Metro High School Advocacy
metadata.metaDescription: 0 curbside pickups. 14 drop-off sites. 100% our responsibility. Help Metro High students educate STL.

popup.message: 🚨 Important Update: Residential recycling is no longer being picked up in many STL neighborhoods. To ensure your items are recycled, they must be taken to an official drop-off station.

hero.title: Recycle St. Louis
hero.tagline: A student advocacy project bringing awareness to the state of recycling in our city.

interview.label: Exclusive Interview
interview.title: Talking Policy with Mayor Cara Spencer
interview.description: We sat down with St. Louis Mayor Cara Spencer to discuss the suspension of curbside recycling and what the city's long-term plan is for sustainable waste management.

article.title: The Curbside Crisis
article.body: Due to labor shortages and high contamination rates, St. Louis has officially pivoted to a 'Drop-Off' model. Placing recyclables in your alley bin now almost guarantees they end up in a landfill.

entries.label: Logistics & Waste

entries.description: We tour the local facility to see the sorting process in action.

questions.q: Is a greasy pizza box recyclable in STL?
questions.info: Grease ruins paper. Recycle only the clean lid!

questions.q: Can plastic bags go in blue bins?
questions.info: They jam sorting machines. Return them to grocery stores.

questions.q: Do you need to rinse out food jars?
questions.info: Residue contaminates batches. Give it a quick scrub!

questions.q: Should you leave the caps on plastic bottles?
questions.info: Keep them on! Modern machines capture them better that way.

questions.q: Is shredded paper okay for the blue bin?
questions.info: Small bits fall through equipment. Compost it instead.

questions.q: Can you recycle mirrors or lightbulbs in the bin?
questions.info: This glass has different melting points and ruins the batch.

questions.q: Does STL currently have curbside pickup city-wide?
questions.info: Most residents must use drop-off stations.
